explicit_text: I was born and did my internship and residency in internal medicine in Rochester, New York. It was there that I trained fully before entering the Army, which was a privilege because it allowed me to avoid field work. Rochester was really the foundation of my medical career before I deployed to Vietnam.

That city was really the foundation of my medical career before I deployed to Vietnam.

explicit_text: I was granted the privilege of entering the Berry Plan, which gave me a full deferment until I finished my medical training. Thanks to that, I was able to go into the Army fully trained as an internist, avoiding the more dangerous field assignments. This plan really shaped my military medical career starting in 1970.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Army at age 32 after completing my residency. I started as a captain and was promoted to major shortly after arriving in Vietnam. The Army gave me the opportunity to serve as an internist at the 85th Evacuation Hospital, where I treated soldiers and supported other units during the Vietnam War.

explicit_text: I was stationed at Phu Bai from August 1970 to August 1971, working at the 85th Evacuation Hospital. It was a challenging environment, but we treated many soldiers and supported units like the 101st Airborne Division. Phu Bai was really the center of my Vietnam War medical experience.
implicit_text: I was stationed at a key medical base near Hue from August 1970 to August 1971, working at the 85th Evacuation Hospital. It was a challenging environment, but we treated many soldiers and supported units like the 101st Airborne Division. That base was really the center of my Vietnam War medical experience.

explicit_text: At the 85th Evacuation Hospital in Phu Bai, I worked with two other physicians managing three wards and a clinic. We treated infections, malaria, hepatitis, and drug-related issues among soldiers, and supported other units like the 101st Airborne. It was a busy and close-knit team that handled everything from routine care to mass casualty events.

explicit_text: Before deploying to Vietnam, I completed six weeks of basic training in San Antonio in mid-1970. It was the final step before shipping out to the war zone. San Antonio was where I transitioned from civilian medicine to military service.

explicit_text: I served as a medical officer during the Vietnam War from 1970 to 1971, stationed primarily at Phu Bai. The war presented many challenges, from treating infectious diseases to managing mass casualties from combat. It was a time of rapid medical advances, especially with helicopter evacuations and trauma care.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in Vietnam in August 1970, we landed at Tan Son Nhut Air Base. The heat and pungent smells there were striking and made me question if I was in the right place. It was a busy hub before we flew on to Da Nang and then Phu Bai.
implicit_text: When I first arrived in Vietnam in August 1970, we landed at the main international airport near Saigon. The heat and pungent smells there were striking and made me question if I was in the right place. It was a busy hub before we flew on to Da Nang and then to my station near Hue.

explicit_text: After landing at Tan Son Nhut, we flew to Da Nang and then on to Phu Bai. Da Nang was a key transit point for troops and supplies during the war. It was part of the journey that brought me to my station at the 85th Evacuation Hospital.

explicit_text: The 85th Evacuation Hospital supported various units, including the 101st Airborne Division. We treated soldiers from that division when they had medical issues beyond their unit's capabilities. It was important to provide specialized care to support their operations.

explicit_text: Mike Grossman was one of my medical associates at the 85th Evacuation Hospital. He and another colleague started a drug rehabilitation program to help soldiers struggling with drug abuse. Their work was pioneering at a time when the Army largely denied the extent of the drug problem.

Their work was pioneering at a time when the military largely denied the extent of the drug problem.

explicit_text: We occasionally visited the hospital and clinics in Hue to offer medical expertise. Some of the patients there had tuberculosis and other diseases, but we mostly treated soldiers at our base. Hue was one of the few places where we interacted with Vietnamese civilians medically.

explicit_text: I wasn't directly involved in MEDCAP missions, which are medical civic action programs to treat villagers. Some corpsmen and battalion surgeons from firebases did that work, but we mostly treated military personnel at Eagle Base. Our clinic was closed to the general Vietnamese population.

explicit_text: Eagle Base was where our clinic was located, and it was primarily for base personnel. We didn't have an open clinic for Vietnamese civilians, so our interactions with locals were limited. It was a secured environment focused on supporting soldiers.

explicit_text: Mass casualty events were intense and demanding. I remember one on Christmas night when a friendly-fire incident brought in about 25 wounded soldiers. Everyone scrambled to triage and treat patients through the night, showing how critical teamwork was in those moments.

explicit_text: Early on, I helped treat a soldier who was burned by JP-4 fuel, even though it wasn't my specialty. We all pitched in to manage injuries and emergencies regardless of our specific roles. JP-4 burns were one of the many hazards we encountered in Vietnam.

explicit_text: Christmas in Vietnam was memorable, especially because of a mass casualty event from friendly fire that night. Instead of celebrating, we worked through the night treating about 25 wounded soldiers. It was a stark reminder of the war's harsh realities even during holidays.

explicit_text: Dustoff helicopter evacuations were a game changer in trauma care during the Vietnam War. Whenever we heard a chopper, we knew casualties were coming in and had to be ready. The speed of dustoff greatly improved survival rates by getting wounded soldiers to care within the golden hour.
implicit_text: Helicopter evacuations during the war were a game changer in trauma care. Whenever we heard a chopper, we knew casualties were coming in and had to be ready. The speed of these evacuations greatly improved survival rates by getting wounded soldiers to care within the golden hour.

explicit_text: The concept of the golden hour was very real in Vietnam. Thanks to rapid helicopter evacuations, soldiers wounded in firebases could reach our hospital within 20 to 30 minutes. This quick response dramatically increased survival rates compared to slower ground transport.

explicit_text: I was able to take R&R in Hawaii during my Vietnam tour, which was a welcome break from the war zone. Hawaii also had Tripler Army Medical Center, a key facility for soldiers recovering from injuries. My trips there helped me recharge before returning to duty.

explicit_text: Japan was a frequent destination for medevacs of seriously injured soldiers from Vietnam. I accompanied some patients there during transport. The medical facilities in Japan served as important staging points before soldiers returned to the United States or other locations.

explicit_text: Okinawa was another key location where injured soldiers were taken after evacuation from Vietnam. It served as a medical hub along with Japan before patients were sent back to the States. These stops were critical in the chain of care for wounded troops.

explicit_text: The 6th Convalescent Hospital was a facility where soldiers who couldn't return to duty were sent after initial treatment. It acted as a staging area before some were flown back to the United States. It was part of the continuum of care beyond the combat zone.

explicit_text: Tripler Army Medical Center in Hawaii was a major medical facility for soldiers recovering from Vietnam injuries. While some patients were flown directly back to the States, Tripler was an important destination for many. I personally visited Hawaii but did not accompany patients there.

explicit_text: We used Lockheed C-141 Starlifter aircraft to transport injured soldiers from Vietnam to Japan or the United States. These planes were improvised for medical use with basic oxygen and IV setups but were far from the modern flying ICUs seen in later conflicts. They were essential for long-distance medical evacuation.

They were essential for long-distance medical evacuation.

explicit_text: Though I did not serve in the Iraq War, I have seen how medical evacuation and care have advanced dramatically since Vietnam. The flying ICUs and operating rooms in Iraq are far more sophisticated than what we had in the 1970s. It's remarkable how military medicine has evolved.

explicit_text: Similar to the Iraq War, the War in Afghanistan saw huge improvements in trauma care and medical evacuation. The technology and facilities now are almost indistinguishable from modern hospitals, a far cry from the improvised setups we had in Vietnam. It's a testament to how far military medicine has come.
